title: Prefix Sum
description: Precomputing running totals to answer any range-sum query in O(1), after building the array once in O(n).
objectives: Build a prefix sum array from an input array
Answer range-sum queries in O(1) using the prefix sum array
Recognize when prefix sum beats repeatedly summing a range directly

sections.text: Suppose we need to answer MANY questions like "what's the sum from index 2 to index 5?" on the SAME array, over and over. Summing the range directly each time is wasteful if we're asked this hundreds of times.
Each call to rangeSumDirect is O(range length). If we call this q times on an array of size n, total work is O(q * n) in the worst case. Prefix sum precomputes once so each query becomes O(1).
An array where each position holds the sum of all original elements up to and including that position, allowing any range sum to be computed with a simple subtraction.
prefix[i] represents "the sum of everything from index 0 to index i." This is built in a single O(n) pass, using the same running-total idea from the Sliding Window lesson's windowSum.
3. Answering a Range Sum in O(1)

prefix[right] - prefix[left - 1] works because prefix[right] is 'everything up to right', and prefix[left - 1] is 'everything up to just before left' -- subtracting removes exactly the part we don't want, leaving only arr[left..right]. The left == 0 special case exists because prefix[-1] doesn't exist -- there's nothing to subtract when the range starts at the very beginning.

sections.title: Direct summation vs prefix sum, for q queries on n elements
Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting the special case for left == 0 -- prefix[left - 1] would be prefix[-1], which doesn't exist and throws an exception.
Off-by-one confusion between prefix[i] meaning 'sum up to AND INCLUDING i' versus 'sum up to but not including i' -- this lesson uses the inclusive convention consistently.
Rebuilding the prefix array for every single query instead of building it ONCE and reusing it -- that would defeat the entire purpose.

Using prefix sum when the array is being frequently MODIFIED between queries -- a static prefix array becomes stale after any update to the original array, requiring a rebuild or a different data structure entirely.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a prefix sum array?
questions.answer: An array where each position holds the running total of all original elements up to and including that position, allowing any range sum to be computed quickly via subtraction.

questions.question: How do you compute the sum of arr[left..right] using a prefix sum array?
questions.answer: prefix[right] - prefix[left - 1], handling the special case where left == 0 by just returning prefix[right] directly, since there's no prefix[-1].

questions.question: Why is prefix sum faster than direct summation for answering many range-sum queries?
questions.answer: Prefix sum spends O(n) once to build the array, after which every query is answered in O(1) via subtraction. Direct summation costs up to O(n) per query, so for q queries prefix sum is O(n + q) total versus O(q * n) for direct summation.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A prefix sum array stores running totals -- prefix[i] holds the sum of all elements from the start through index i -- built in a single O(n) pass. Once built, any range sum arr[left..right] can be answered in O(1) via prefix[right] - prefix[left - 1] (with a special case when left is 0), since subtracting removes exactly the unwanted portion. This turns q repeated range-sum queries from O(q*n) with direct summation into O(n + q) total, at the cost of needing to rebuild the prefix array if the underlying data changes.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: prefix[i] = "everything from the start THROUGH i." Range sum = big total MINUS the part you don't want: prefix[right] - prefix[left-1]. Build ONCE, query FOREVER in O(1).

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What does prefix[i] represent in a prefix sum array?
How do you compute the sum of a range [left, right] using a prefix sum array?
Why is there a special case when left == 0?
Why is prefix sum better than direct summation for many repeated queries?

sections.title: Running Sum of 1d Array
sections.problemStatement: Given an array nums, return the running sum of nums, where runningSum[i] = sum(nums[0]...nums[i]).
hints: This IS the prefix sum array itself -- no separate range queries needed, just return the running total array directly.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For each index i, sum every element from 0 to i using a nested loop.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Build the prefix sum in a single pass, exactly as shown in this lesson's Section 2.

sections.title: Find Pivot Index
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of integers nums, return the leftmost pivot index -- the index where the sum of all values to its left equals the sum of all values to its right (0 if there's nothing on that side). If no such index exists, return -1.

examples.explanation: Left sum = 1+7+3 = 11, right sum = 5+6 = 11 -- both equal 11.

hints: The total sum is fixed. If you know the sum of everything to the LEFT of index i, the right sum is just totalSum - leftSum - nums[i].
You don't need a full prefix sum array -- just track a running left sum as you scan once.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For each index, recompute the left sum and right sum from scratch by scanning both sides.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Compute the total sum once, then scan left to right maintaining a running left sum, deriving the right sum by subtraction.

sections.title: Range Sum Query - Immutable
sections.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums, handle multiple queries of the following type: calculate the sum of the elements of nums between indices left and right inclusive. Implement the NumArray class with a constructor, and a sumRange(left, right) method.
hints: This is precisely the multi-query prefix sum pattern from this lesson's Section 3 -- build the prefix array ONCE in the constructor, answer each query in O(1).

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Store the array as-is; sum the requested range directly on every sumRange call.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Precompute a prefix sum array once in the constructor; each sumRange call becomes O(1) via subtraction.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n) once in the constructor, O(1) per sumRange call

sections.title: Find the Highest Altitude
sections.problemStatement: There is a biker going on a road trip with n+1 points at different altitudes. You are given an integer array gain of length n where gain[i] is the net gain in altitude between point i and point (i+1). The biker starts at altitude 0. Return the highest altitude reached.
hints: The altitude at point i is exactly a running (prefix) sum of gain[0..i-1], starting from 0.
You don't need to store every altitude -- just track the current altitude and the maximum seen so far.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Build the full altitude array (a genuine prefix sum with a leading 0), then scan it for the maximum.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Track the current altitude and running maximum in a single pass, without storing the full altitude array.

sections.title: Find the Middle Index in Array
sections.problemStatement: Given a 0-indexed integer array nums, find the leftmost middleIndex where the sum of all elements strictly to its left equals the sum of all elements strictly to its right. If no such index exists, return -1. (This is the same underlying problem as Find Pivot Index, reframed.)
hints: Identical technique to Find Pivot Index: total sum minus running left sum minus the current element gives the right sum in O(1) per index.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For every index, recompute both side sums by scanning.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Same running-sum-with-subtraction technique as Find Pivot Index -- total sum computed once, then a single left-to-right pass.

sections.title: Product of Array Except Self
sections.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums, return an array answer such that answer[i] is equal to the product of all the elements of nums except nums[i]. You must write an algorithm that runs in O(n) time without using the division operator.
constraints: The product of any prefix or suffix of nums is guaranteed to fit in a 32-bit integer
hints: answer[i] = (product of everything to the LEFT of i) * (product of everything to the RIGHT of i).
This is exactly a prefix product (left to right) combined with a suffix product (right to left) -- the same running-total idea as prefix SUM, just with multiplication.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For each index, multiply every other element directly -- uses no division, but is quadratic.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Build a prefix-product pass left-to-right, then fold in a suffix-product pass right-to-left, combining both into the answer array in O(n) with O(1) extra space beyond the output.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space (beyond the required output array)
